14: exaleipsas to kath' hemon cheirographon tois dogmasin ho en hypenantios hemin (exaleipsas to kath' hēmōn cheirographon tois dogmasin ho ēn hypenantios hēmin): 'having canceled the record of debt (cheirographon) that stood against us with its legal demands.' Cheirographon (handwritten document/bond) was a legal IOU — a debtor's acknowledgment of what was owed. The metaphor: humanity's accumulated debt of sin-obligations constituted a document that indicted us. proseilosas auto to staurō: 'nailing it to the cross.' The practice of affixing a notice of charges (titulus) to the cross of a condemned person (John 19:19-22: 'Jesus of Nazareth, King of the Jews') provides the image: our indicting debt-document was nailed to the cross with Christ, absorbing its condemnation in his death.
15: apekdysamenos tas archas kai tas exousias edeigmatisen en parresia thriambeusas autous en auto (apekdysamenos tas archas kai tas exousias edeigmatisen en parrēsia, thriambeusas autous en autō): 'disarming the rulers and authorities and putting them to open shame, by triumphing over them in him.' The cosmic powers (archai kai exousiai) that featured in the Colossian false teaching as spiritual intermediaries are publicly stripped of their authority by the cross. The triumphal procession image: in a Roman triumph, defeated enemies were displayed as captives behind the general's chariot. Christ's cross is the paradoxical triumph: precisely in what looks like defeat, the powers are disarmed and publicly humiliated.
1: Hēlikon agōna echō hyper hymōn kai tōn en Laodikeia kai hosoi ouch heōrakasin to prosōpon mou en sarki (how great a struggle I have for you and for those in Laodicea and however many have not seen my face in the flesh): agōna (struggle/contest) is the athletic and military contest term used for prayerful contending (cf. Colossians 4:12: Epaphras wrestling in prayer). Paul's concern for communities he has not personally visited is distinctive — he acts as a universal apostle, not merely a local pastor.
2: Hina paraklēthōsin hai kardiai autōn, symbibasthentas en agapē kai eis panta ploutos tēs plērophorias tēs syneseōs, eis epignōsin tou mystēriou tou theou, Christou (so that their hearts may be encouraged, being knit together in love and into all the wealth of full assurance of understanding, into knowledge of the mystery of God, which is Christ): symbibasthentas en agapē (knit together in love) — symbibazo is used in 2:19 of the body being joined; here the community is the body being knit. Plērophorias (full assurance/certainty) contrasts with the speculative uncertainty the false teachers may have promoted.
3: En hō eisin pantes hoi thēsauroi tēs sophias kai gnōseōs apokryphoi (in whom are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge): thēsauroi (treasures) — the full deposit of wisdom is concentrated in Christ. Apokryphoi (hidden) — a wordplay: the mystery once hidden from the ages (Colossians 1:26) is now revealed in Christ, yet wisdom remains 'hidden' in him in the sense that only union with Christ unlocks it. This preempts any claim to additional esoteric wisdom beyond Christ.
4: Hina mēdeis hymas paralogizētai en pithanologia (so that no one may reason you astray by persuasive argument): paralogizētai (reason astray) — para- prefix signals deviation from correct reasoning. Pithanologia (persuasive speech) is a rhetoric term; the false teachers' arguments are outwardly compelling but internally specious. The pastoral goal is to inoculate the community against subtle intellectual seduction.
5: Tēn taxin kai to stereōma tēs eis Christon pisteōs hymōn (your order and the firmness of your faith in Christ): taxin (order/arrangement) is a military term for ranks in formation; stereōma (firmness/solid foundation) is either the un-breached state of a formation or the fortress wall resisting assault. Paul's military metaphor frames the Colossians as a community holding a position against the errorists' attack.
6: Hōs oun parelabete ton Christon Iēsoun ton Kyrion, en autō peripateitei (just as you received Christ Jesus the Lord, walk in him): parelabete (received) — the technical term for receiving authoritative tradition (paradosis); the same verb as 1 Corinthians 11:23 (receiving the Lord's Supper tradition). The implication: the teaching they received from Epaphras is the authoritative tradition; any supplementation is deviation. Peripateitei (walk) — ethical conduct shaped by the received gospel.
7: Errizōmenoi kai epoikodomoumeoi en autō kai bebaioumenoi tē pistei kathōs edidachthēte, perisseuontes en [tē] eucharistia (having been rooted and being built up in him and confirmed in the faith as you were taught, overflowing in thanksgiving): the mixed metaphor of root (agricultural, perfect: already fixed) and building (architectural, present: ongoing) is characteristic Paul (cf. 1 Corinthians 3:9-15). Perisseuontes en tē eucharistia (overflowing in thanksgiving) — the community's surplus response to grace received.
8: Sylagōgōn dia tēs philosophias kai kenēs apatēs kata tēn paradosin tōn anthrōpōn, kata ta stoicheia tou kosmou kai ou kata Christon (plundering you through philosophy and empty deceit according to human tradition, according to the elemental powers of the world and not according to Christ): stoicheia tou kosmou (elemental powers of the world) — most coherent in context (Colossians 2:20: died with Christ to the stoicheia; Colossians 2:15: cosmic powers disarmed) as spiritual forces governing the cosmic order, to whose regulations the false teachers' calendar and dietary system is tied. Ou kata Christon (not according to Christ) — the decisive criterion: any teaching not measured by Christ is empty deceit.
9: Hoti en autō katoikei pan to plērōma tēs theotētos sōmatikōs (because in him dwells all the fullness of the Godhead bodily): theotētos (of the Godhead/Deity) is the abstract noun of divinity itself — distinct from theiotētos (Godlike quality, Romans 1:20) which denotes divine characteristics. Theotētos means the full ontological being of God. Sōmatikōs (bodily) counters any distribution of the fullness among angelic intermediaries: all divine fullness is concentrated in the incarnate body of Christ.
10: Kai este en autō peplērōmenoi, hos estin hē kephalē pasēs archēs kai exousias (and you are in him having been made full, who is the head of every ruler and authority): peplērōmenoi (having been filled/made complete) — perfect passive: the filling is complete and its effects remain. The Colossians already have the fullness that is in Christ; no additional spiritual supplement is needed. Kephalē pasēs archēs kai exousias (head of every ruler and authority) — Christ's headship over the cosmic powers renders their independent veneration unnecessary.
11: Peritomē acheiropoiētō en tē apekdysei tou sōmatos tēs sarkos, en tē peritomē tou Christou (circumcision not made with hands in the stripping off of the body of flesh, in the circumcision of Christ): acheiropoiētos (not made with hands) — Paul's contrast between physical ritual and its eschatological fulfillment (cf. 2 Corinthians 5:1; Hebrews 9:11). Apekdysis tou sōmatos tēs sarkos (stripping off the body of flesh) — the circumcision metaphor becomes a clothing/unclothing metaphor; the deep circumcision is union with Christ's death.
12: Syntaphentes autō en tō baptismati, en hō kai synēgerthēte dia tēs pisteōs tēs energeias tou theou tou egeirantos auton ek tōn nekrōn (having been buried with him in baptism, in which you were also raised with him through faith in the working of God who raised him from the dead): baptism as death and resurrection — Romans 6's participatory logic applied here. Notably, in Colossians the resurrection is stated in the past tense (synēgerthēte), a more realized eschatology than Romans 6:5 ('we will be united' — future), suggesting a developed Christological framing of baptism.
13: Kai hymas nekrous ontas [en] tois paraptōmasin kai tē akrobystia tēs sarkos hymōn, synezōopoiēsen hymas syn autō, charissamenos hēmin panta ta paraptōmata (you, being dead in your transgressions and the uncircumcision of your flesh, he made alive together with him, having forgiven all the transgressions): nekrous ontas en tois paraptōmasin (being dead in transgressions) — Ephesians 2:1 has nearly identical language. Charissamenos (having forgiven/graciously given) — from charizomai, cognate with charis (grace): the forgiveness is a gracious gift.
16: Mē oun tis hymas krinetō en brōsei kai en posei ē en merei heortēs ē neomēnias ē sabbatōn (therefore let no one judge you in eating and drinking or in respect to a feast or new moon or Sabbaths): food/drink restrictions and the Jewish calendar (annual feasts, monthly new moons, weekly Sabbaths) indicate a Jewish or Jewish-syncretic element in the Colossian teaching. The particular prohibitions suggest a system of dietary purity tied to the observance of cosmic-calendar time-units under angelic governance.
17: Ha estin skia tōn mellontōn, to de sōma tou Christou (which are a shadow of the things to come, but the body is of Christ): skia/sōma (shadow/body) is a Platonic-Hellenistic distinction between the shadowy copy and the real substance, adapted for Christological typology. The OT institutions are the shadow cast by the coming reality; Christ is the substance (sōma) that casts the shadow. This is a positive typological reading, not mere abrogation.
18: Katabrabeuetō thelōn en tapeinophrosunē kai thrēskeia tōn angelōn, ha heōraken embateuōn, eikē physiōmenos hypo tou noos tēs sarkos autou (let no one disqualify you, delighting in humility and worship of angels, entering into what he has seen, being inflated in vain by the mind of the flesh): katabrabeuetō (disqualify as umpire/judge) — the false teacher acts as an unauthorized umpire. Thrēskeia tōn angelōn (worship of angels) — whether angels are worshipped or the teachers are performing angel-like worship, the common element is veneration of angelic powers. Embateuōn — used in mystery religion contexts for entering sacred space after initiation, suggesting visionary or mystical access claims.
19: Kai ou kratōn tēn kephalēn, ex hou pan to sōma dia tōn haphōn kai syndesmōn epichorēgoumenon kai symbibazomenon auxei tēn auxēsin tou theou (not holding to the head, from whom the whole body, supplied and knit together through the joints and ligaments, grows with God's growth): the false teacher has severed connection with Christ the head. The anatomical body-image: haphōn kai syndesmōn (joints and ligaments) hold the body together. The church grows only by maintaining connection to Christ — all other spiritual programs bypass the head and disrupt organic growth.
20: Ei apethanete syn Christō apo tōn stoicheiōn tou kosmou, ti hōs zōntes en kosmō dogmatizesthe (if you died with Christ from the elemental powers of the world, why, as though living in the world, do you submit to regulations): co-death with Christ (cf. Colossians 2:12; Colossians 3:3; Romans 6:2-7) breaks legal obligation to the old powers — death dissolves contractual obligation (cf. Romans 7:1-4). Dogmatizesthe (you submit to decrees) — the cognitive power of the stoicheia's regulatory system is challenged: you have died to it; why live as though still under it?
21: Mē hapsē mēde geusē mēde thigēs (do not handle, do not taste, do not touch): Paul quotes the errorists' regulations in compressed form — three prohibitions of tactile/gustatory contact in ascending restriction. The triple prohibition caricatures the dietary and purity regime by presenting it in its bare regulatory form, divorced from any theological justification.
22: Ha estin panta eis phthoran tē apochrēsei, kata ta entalmata kai didaskalias tōn anthrōpōn (which are all things unto corruption in use, according to human commandments and teachings): eis phthoran tē apochrēsei — the prohibited things are perishable: food decays on digestion. Paul echoes Mark 7:19 (food cannot defile) and Jesus' contrast between internal and external defilement. Entalmata kai didaskalias tōn anthrōpōn (commandments and teachings of humans) — Isaiah 29:13 LXX (cited in Mark 7:7): the errorists' regulations have the status of the human traditions Jesus condemned.
23: Hatina estin logon men echonta sophias en ethelothrēskia kai tapeinophrosunē kai apheidia sōmatos, ouk en timē tini pros plēsmonēn tēs sarkos (which have a reputation of wisdom in self-imposed worship and humility and severity to the body, not in any honor against the satisfaction of the flesh): ethelothrēskia (self-imposed worship) — a hapax; a compound of voluntary will (thelō) and worship (thrēskeia). Apheidia sōmatos (harsh treatment of the body) — impressive asceticism. Paul's verdict: it has the appearance of wisdom but no actual power against fleshly indulgence — the very problem it claims to solve, it does not solve.
